retrieved_docs.title: Albinism -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Waardenburg syndrome type II: Autosomal dominant MITF gene mutation presenting with patchy skin hypopigmentation, white forelock or prematurely gray hair, iris heterochromia, sensorineural hearing loss

retrieved_docs.title: Albinism -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: OCA2 Classic OCA2: Skin is creamy white (not stark white like in OCA1) or tan skin, frequently with nevi and freckles. Hair, eyebrows, and eyelashes are yellow, blond, or light brown. Irides are blue, hazel, brown or gray. Visual acuity ranges from 20/25 to 20/200, usually 20/60 to 20/100. Brown OCA2: Subjects appear normal with brown skin, hair, and eyes, but are relatively hypopigmented compared to family members Red OCA2: This variant occurs with a concomitant MC1R gene mutation. Subjects have light-colored eyes and vision problems; however, they typically have red hair.

retrieved_docs.title: Albinism -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Cutaneous findings vary widely, but the most dramatic features occur in OCA1A due to complete lack of melanin.  The white hair, white eyelashes, white skin, and pink eyes are typical of what the general populations consider an albino.  Some darkening over time from shampoos and water minerals may occur but spares the eyelashes and eyebrows.  Every other form of albinism has some residual pigmentation that can increase with time, mostly through pheomelanin.  For this reason, comparison with family members may be vital in raising the clinical suspicion of albinism, especially if ocular manifestations are mild. Relative hypopigmentation in Caucasian families is more difficult to appreciate than in families with more pigment. Ocular findings are the more debilitating clinical feature due to its effect on vision; they can be divided into refractive and nonrefractive errors.

retrieved_docs.title: Loose Anagen Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A pull test is the cornerstone of diagnosis in loose anagen hair syndrome. It is performed by using gentle traction on approximately 40 to 60 scalp hairs. Telogen hairs normally represent 10% of scalp hairs, and thus, removal of 4 to 6 hairs is a normal finding in the hair pull test. Loose anagen hair syndrome, similar to telogen effluvium, will result in greater amounts of hair removed. Despite easy removal from the scalp, individual hairs in loose anagen hair syndrome have normal tensile strength and are not brittle. Hairs can be examined under light microscopy by dry-mounting the hair on a glass slide with a cover slip placed over top. In loose anagen hair syndrome anagen, hairs lack inner root sheaths and external root sheaths with a ruffled cuticle. [5] As loose hairs may be seen in unaffected individuals some propose a positive hair test contain 50% or more of anagen hairs. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetic Hearing Loss -- Pathophysiology -- Autosomal Dominant Syndromic Hearing Loss
retrieved_docs.content: Waardenburg syndrome has an incidence of 1 in 40,000 live births and consists of SNHL and abnormal pigmentation of the eyes, skin, and hair. Patients can have the classic “white forelock” and iris heterochromia. [18] There are four subtypes of Waardenburg syndrome with slight variations in clinical features and different gene mutations.

retrieved_docs.title: Piebaldism -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Piebaldism is an autosomal dominant disorder affecting melanocyte migration and development characterized by isolated congenital leukoderma (white skin) and poliosis (white hair) in a distinct ventral midline pattern. The most common cause of piebaldism is a mutation in the KIT proto-oncogene. However, researchers have identified other mutations. [1] This mutation leads to abnormal melanocyte migration from the neural crest leading to leukoderma affecting the central forehead, central frontal scalp, mid-portion of the extremities, and central anterior trunk. Poliosis affecting a lock of hair just above the forehead is the most common finding in this disease. Histopathologic examination of the affected areas reveals an absence of melanocytes in the epidermis and hair follicles. If the diagnosis of piebaldism is suspected, a careful examination should take place to exclude overlapping syndromes with extra-cutaneous manifestations, such as Waardenburg syndrome. [1] Although benign and isolated to the skin, piebaldism can be socially disabling for affected individuals. Treatment is targeted to improve cosmesis, including skin grafting, cell transplantation, camouflage techniques, and the use of hair dye for poliosis. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Piebaldism -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Piebaldism is a disorder of melanocyte migration and development A white forelock is present in approximately 90% of cases It is crucial to rule out associated syndromes at birth (e.g., Waardenburg) Treatment options include camouflage techniques, hair dyes, skin grafting, and cell transplantation

retrieved_docs.title: Waardenburg Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Waardenburg syndrome comprises a group of genetic disorders resulting from mutations in genes that impair the migration and differentiation of neural crest cells. This condition typically presents with varying degrees of congenital sensorineural hearing loss and pigmentary abnormalities of the skin, hair, and eyes. Waardenburg syndrome is classified into 4 types, each distinguished by specific clinical features and underlying genetic mutations. A positive family history is the primary risk factor, reflecting the autosomal dominant or, less commonly, autosomal recessive inheritance patterns associated with different subtypes. Diagnosis is primarily based on clinical evaluation, supported by audiological assessments and, when available, molecular genetic testing. Management emphasizes early hearing rehabilitation, regular ophthalmological monitoring, and cosmetic support for pigmentation abnormalities. Prognosis varies based on the specific type and severity of the condition; however, with appropriate intervention, most individuals maintain normal life expectancy and developmental potential.

retrieved_docs.title: Alopecia Totalis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic predisposition has been supported by genome-wide associated studies identifying certain human leukocyte antigen (HLA) genes associated with the development of AA. HLA-DRB1 polymorphisms have increased susceptibility for AA, specifically HLA-DRB1*04 and HLA-DRB1*16. [5] HLA-DQB1*03 has also been found to be associated with AA. A few polymorphisms have been identified that are more highly associated with AT and AU than AF; these include HLA-DRB1*1104 and HLA-DQB1*0301. [6] [7] In addition to genetic influence, environmental factors such as illness, infection, drugs, and stress may contribute to flares or relapse of disease. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Albinism -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Tietz albinism-deafness syndrome: Autosomal dominant MITF gene mutation presenting with white eyebrows and eyelashes, iris hypopigmentation, normal visual acuity, and sensorineural hearing loss

retrieved_docs.title: Albinism -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Chediak-Higashi syndrome (CHS): Subjects have partial OCA (hypopigmented skin, gray or silver-sheen hair, nystagmus, foveal hypoplasia, photophobia). All subjects exhibit increased susceptibility to pyogenic infections, neutropenia, peripheral neuropathy, mild coagulopathy.

retrieved_docs.title: Albinism -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: OCA3: This condition, however, mostly affects dark-skinned individuals where it may be brown or “rufous” OCA.  The latter is characterized by reddish brown (copper) skin, red or reddish yellow (ginger) hair, hazel or light brown irides, and sometimes undetectable visual impairment.

retrieved_docs.title: Androgenetic Alopecia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Androgenetic alopecia, as indicated by its name, possesses a distinct genetic predisposition and is predominantly due to an excessive response to androgen. [5] [6] This condition is characterized by a polygenic nature with varying penetrance degrees, influenced by maternal and paternal genes. A familial predisposition towards androgenetic alopecia exists, with sons having 5 to 6 times higher relative risk if their fathers experienced balding.

retrieved_docs.title: Albinism -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: OCA4: Similar to classic OCA2 because of pheomelanin predominance. Skin is cream colored to tan to normal. Hair is never stark white; it may be silvery white to light blond to yellow to very light brown.  Skin and hair color are usually correlated. Irides are blue, hazel to light brown. Visual acuity ranges from 20/30 to 20/400; usually 20/100 to 20/200.

retrieved_docs.title: Alopecia Totalis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic and environmental factors contribute to the development of AT. Twin and familial studies support a genetic predisposition to the disease. Studies of identical twins report a concordance rate of 55%. A positive family history of AA is found in about 20% of patients with AT. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Proteins -- Pathophysiology -- Enzymatic
retrieved_docs.content: A genetic disease can cause a deficiency of the enzyme required to convert amino acids into neurotransmitters and skin pigment. The disease does not result from the decrease in amino acid levels but rather from the high concentration of amino acids. This promotes activity in a minor enzymatic pathway, producing toxic products. These products are screened for in the blood as part of standard neonatal testing. Patients usually express traits of albinism, white-blond hair, pale blue eyes, and an odor from the toxic products in their sweat, urine, skin, and hair. Treatment of this disease is with a diet low in specific amino acids. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1795
retrieved_docs.content: The skin disorder حُمامَى is linked to other inflammatory conditions of the skin, indicating that it shares pathogenic mechanisms and clinical associations with additional inflammatory dermatoses. This “associated_with” relationship highlights the relevance of recognizing حُمامَى within the broader spectrum of skin inflammation for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 4 -- Community 2732
retrieved_docs.content: Other specified conditions related to pregnancy comprise a range of distinct gestational disorders. This category includes renal disorder during pregnancy, peripheral neuropathy during pregnancy, and polymorphic eruption of pregnancy, each representing a separate organ‑system complication that can arise in the context of gestation and is classified under the broader label of other specified pregnancy‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 81
retrieved_docs.content: congenital anomalies** contain hereditary and genetic disorders, which also comprise **other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions** and sexually transmitted infections; this hereditary‑genetic group is a member of both **فالج طفلي** and **متلازمة ستيكلر**. Collectively, the integrated structure demonstrates how endocrine, metabolic, fluid‑electrolyte, renal, infectious, vascular, otolaryngologic, behavioral, psychiatric, genetic, and developmental concepts intersect, providing a comprehensive framework for patient assessment, risk stratification, and multidisciplinary clinical management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: فالج is a general medical condition, and فالج طفلي represents a more specific, narrower form of فالج, indicating the infantile variant of this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2460
retrieved_docs.content: التحصّي is associated with other male genital disorders, indicating a connection between this condition and various other disorders affecting the male genitalia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 812
retrieved_docs.content: Burns, referred to in Arabic as حرق حراري, denote thermal injuries to the skin and underlying tissues; the term حرق حراري directly describes the heat‑induced nature of these lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2824
retrieved_docs.content: Genetics is recognized as an allowed qualifier for gout and other crystal arthropathies, for intestinal infection, and for فَرْطُ الدَّرَقِيَّةِ (hyperuricemia). This indicates that hereditary factors may influence susceptibility to these conditions, underscoring the relevance of genetic considerations in their diagnosis, risk assessment, and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1062
retrieved_docs.content: within nervous system disorders. Clinically, شقيقة and شقيقة فالجية عائلية are considered clinically similar, and شقيقة and صداع وعائي are described as related and possibly synonymous, suggesting substantial overlap in their clinical presentation and underlying mechanisms. Together, these relationships integrate migraine into a network of genetic, vascular, and systemic neurological conditions, underscore its diagnostic and therapeutic considerations, and illustrate its connections to both broader headache classifications and specific hereditary migraine phenotypes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2913
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy is a category that includes several distinct conditions. It is classified as الاعتماد على المخدرات، قبل الولادة (drug dependence before delivery). Within this category are the specific complications zwangerschap; vermoeidheid (pregnancy‑related fatigue) and طفح الحمل متعدد الأشكال (multiform rash of pregnancy). These entities represent various adverse events that can occur during gestation, underscoring the importance of comprehensive prenatal assessment and management of substance use, fatigue, and dermatologic manifestations to optimize maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2034
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary congenital anomalies and sexually transmitted infections are both listed as members of the Arabic‑named SMQ “الاضطرابات الخِلقية والعائلية والجينية (SMQ)”, which groups together congenital, familial and genetic disorders. This classification indicates that, within this standardized medical terminology, these two distinct clinical categories are considered part of a broader collection of disorders that share underlying developmental, hereditary, or genetic characteristics. Recognizing their inclusion in the same SMQ can aid clinicians and researchers in organizing diagnostic codes, facilitating epidemiological studies, and ensuring comprehensive patient care that addresses both congenital urogenital conditions and infections transmitted through sexual contact under a unified framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2356
retrieved_docs.content: Other non‑epithelial cancer of skin is a broader classification that encompasses the condition described in Arabic as ورم خبيث في الجلد، آخر, indicating an additional malignant tumor of the skin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2198
retrieved_docs.content: Hereditary disorder of nervous system is a broad category of genetic conditions affecting the nervous system, and شقيقة فالجية عائلية (familial hemiplegic migraine) is a specific instance within this category. شقيقة فالجية عائلية represents a hereditary form of migraine that is characterized by transient hemiplegia and other neurological symptoms, illustrating how a hereditary disorder of the nervous system can manifest as a distinct migraine subtype.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 113
retrieved_docs.content: hydantoin‑derived agents are organized across dermatological, gynecological and systemic therapeutic contexts, highlighting overlapping uses (e.g., haloprogin, selenium sulfide, and terbinafine) and important safety considerations such as the fetal hydantoin syndrome risk associated with hydantoin derivatives.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 683
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the network of relationships portrays diabetes as a multifaceted condition that influences and is influenced by genetic abnormalities, neonatal presentations, limb and ocular complications, metabolic comorbidities, diagnostic testing, and medication safety, all of which are essential considerations for comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 261
retrieved_docs.content: disease classifications, metabolic disorder taxonomy, symptom‑treatment relationships, and genetic inheritance patterns, illustrating how elevated laboratory values, metabolic disturbances, febrile presentations, and overlapping infectious, cardiovascular, endocrine, and gynecologic conditions inform comprehensive patient assessment and care.
